title: CEO Commentary on Loan Condition – As of December 31, 2026
content:
Total loans stood at $6,657,948 thousand as of March 31, 2026, reflecting a modest increase of 0.2% from the previous quarter and remaining flat year-over-year. The portfolio is primarily concentrated in New Vehicle ($1,577,111 thousand) and Used Vehicle loans ($1,425,649 thousand), which together represent nearly half of the total loan balance. Delinquency levels remained stable at $6,752 thousand, or 0.3% of total loans.

Portfolio Composition:
The largest segments by dollar size are New Vehicle and Used Vehicle loans, totaling approximately $3 billion combined for a share of roughly 94%. These two categories dominate the portfolio composition alongside First Lien Real Estate ($1,878,222 thousand) which accounts for nearly one-third of total assets. The remaining smaller segments include All Other Non-Commercial Loans at $81 thousand (0.0%), All Other Secured Non-Real Estate Loans at $931 thousand (0.4%), Payday Alternative Loans at $23,237 thousand (0.5%), Commercial Loans of Credit Not Real Estate Secured at $9,784 thousand (0.6%), Junior Lien Real Estate at $53,950 thousand (0.8%), Leases Receivable at zero ($0), Unsecured Credit Card loans at $12,930 thousand (0.2%), and Student Loans which were not reported.

Delinquency & Credit Quality:
The total delinquent loan balance was $6,752 thousand as of March 31, 2026, representing a slight increase from the prior quarter but remaining flat year-over-year. The largest exposure within this category is concentrated in Used Vehicle loans at $49 thousand (representing approximately 8% of total delinquencies), followed by New Vehicle loans at zero ($0). Other segments such as First Lien Real Estate and Commercial Loans show no reported delinquent balances.

The concentration of current delinquency diverges from the portfolio composition: while Used Vehicles represent a significant portion of both assets and risk, they also account for nearly all observed delinquencies. This suggests that credit quality in this segment requires continued attention underwriting standards and monitoring practices remain focused on maintaining asset protection across these key areas.
